guideline_references: EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Decompensated cirrhosis (2018)
AASLD Practice Guidance: Ascites due to cirrhosis (2021)
Australian consensus recommendations for hepatitis C management
intentional_test_features: De-anchoring / anti-anchoring: the patient has NO known diagnosis and presents de novo with new massive ascites, peripheral oedema and orthopnoea. The sealed answer (first-presentation decompensated cirrhosis, presumed chronic hepatitis C) must be reached from lay symptoms + remote risk factors, never from the clinician's stigmata exam which is sealed.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present after-hours home visit where the clinician palpated ascites (shifting dullness, fluid thrill, tender hepatomegaly), observed stigmata of chronic liver disease, measured vitals and arranged ambulance admission for paracentesis/diuresis. The AI Doctor cannot examine, cannot measure vitals, cannot obtain bloods/ultrasound/paracentesis — it is scored on recognising probable decompensated liver disease + its emergencies and escalating to urgent hospital assessment.

Anchoring trap: bilateral leg oedema + breathlessness invites premature closure on cardiac failure; long-standing fatigue and 'brain fog' invite dismissal as ageing. Both are red herrings the answer key must resist.

T4/T5 ceiling discipline: baseline is T4 (urgent hospital admission, arranged non-emergency ambulance in the source) with explicit T5/000 escalation edges for the patient-reportable emergencies (haematemesis, melaena, new confusion, sudden severe breathlessness).

No-dose discipline: the correct hospital management (diuresis, albumin, paracentesis, later direct-acting antivirals) is clinician/inpatient-administered and sealed in node 12; the AI Doctor must NOT mint a diuretic or antiviral dose.
Source category: GIH (Gastroenterology & Hepatology), source note GIH-034.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-18)
